model_narrative: A farm in the Agreste region plans a single season. It decides how many hectares of each cropping activity to run on each land type, how much livestock to keep under different feeding alternatives, how much of each crop to sell versus consume on-farm, how much family / temporary / permanent labor to use in each month, and how much land to downgrade between types. The goal is to maximize farm income.

sets.name: ps
sets.doc: set of feasible (cropping-activity p, land-type s) pairs (the activity/land combinations with positive crop yield)

params.name: rations
params.doc: livestock rations per feed alternative (cr per head)

params.name: lio
params.doc: land required per livestock feed alternative (ha per head)

params.name: llab
params.doc: labor for livestock feed (man-days per head)

params.name: crev
params.doc: crop revenue time series (cr per ha)

params.name: yield_param
params.doc: crop yields (kg per ha) — Pyomo attribute is model.yield_param ('yield' is a Python keyword)

params.name: ravg
params.doc: average crop revenue = mean over ty of crev(c,ty)

params.name: prdev
params.doc: price deviations for crops (cr per ton)

params.name: pcost
params.doc: cropping technology cost = sum over km of techc(p,km)

params.name: a
params.doc: cropping constant (0 if activity grows cotton-h, else 1)

vars.name: xcrop
vars.doc: cropping activities (ha); ub xcropl where set

vars.name: revenue
vars.doc: revenue from crop and livestock sales (cr)

constraints.name: sc:
constraints.intent: For each land type, the total area used cannot exceed the area of that type that is available. Land gets used in three ways: by growing crops on it, which only applies to land types suitable for cropping, by land that has been downgraded or converted in from another land type, and by land set aside to feed livestock.

constraints.name: lbal
constraints.intent: Total livestock raised equals the amount of livestock kept, summed across all the available feeding alternatives.

constraints.name: rliv
constraints.intent: The farm's total livestock ration requirement equals, summed over the feeding alternatives, the per-head ration cost of each alternative times the amount of livestock kept on that alternative.

constraints.name: yield_param[p,c,s]
constraints.intent: For each crop, the quantity produced must be at least the quantity used. Production is the crop's yield gathered across all cropping activities and land types, and usage is the amount of that crop sold plus the amount the family consumes through its consumption bundles.

constraints.name: xprod[c]
constraints.intent: For each crop, the total production in tons is the crop's yield summed over every cropping activity and land type.

constraints.name: labor[p,tm]
constraints.intent: For each month, the labor required cannot exceed the labor available that month. Labor is required by cropping activities, based on each activity's monthly labor need per hectare applied over the land it occupies, and by livestock feeding. The labor available that month is family labor plus temporary labor, plus permanent labor converted into man-days.

constraints.name: cond
constraints.intent: The family's on-farm consumption across the bundle alternatives must add up to exactly one full bundle.

constraints.name: prdev[c,ty]
constraints.intent: For each year, the revenue effect of that year's crop price deviations is the difference between a positive-deviation amount and a negative-deviation amount for that year. The revenue effect is each crop's price deviation for the year times the amount of that crop sold, summed over crops.

constraints.name: arev
constraints.intent: Total revenue is income from livestock sales plus income from crop sales. Livestock sales income is the livestock price times the number of head sold. Crop sales income is each crop's reference price times the amount of that crop sold, summed over crops.

constraints.name: acrop
constraints.intent: Total cropping cost is defined as, summed over every feasible pairing of a cropping activity with a land type, the activity's per-hectare technology cost times the hectares of that activity grown on that land.

constraints.name: alab
constraints.intent: Total labor cost is the cost of family labor and of temporary labor, each priced at its own wage rate and summed across the months, then converted from man-days to man-months, plus the cost of permanent labor at its own annual wage.

constraints.name: awcc
constraints.intent: The farm's cash spending during the season cannot exceed the available working capital. The cash items are cropping cost, livestock ration cost, veterinary cost, the wages paid for temporary labor across the months, and the wages paid for permanent labor. Family labor is not a cash outlay, so it is excluded here.

constraints.name: avet
constraints.intent: Total veterinary cost is defined as the per-head veterinary service cost times the number of livestock.

constraints.name: income
constraints.intent: Farm income is revenue, plus the value of the food the family consumes itself, minus labor cost, ration cost, veterinary cost, and cropping cost, minus a risk penalty proportional to the average size of the yearly price deviations, counting positive and negative together, taken across all the years.

query: We're evaluating a diversification rule: no single crop's production output should exceed 20% of total crop tonnage. What does the optimizer return for income and the cropping mix?

model_narrative: A farm in the Agreste region plans a single season. It decides how many hectares of each cropping activity to run on each land type, how much livestock to keep under different feeding alternatives, how much of each crop to sell versus consume on-farm, how much family / temporary / permanent labor to use in each month, and how much land to downgrade between types. The goal is to maximize farm income.

query: The board is pitching a staffing rule that ties the permanent crew to the size of the herd — they want at least one full-time permanent worker on the books for every four head of livestock the operation runs. We're looking at what that does to the books. What does the optimizer return for farm income and the plan?

query: There's a proposal on the table to set a household food-production floor: across all the crops together, the farm should bring at least 10 tons of harvest out of the ground each season before we even think about what gets sold versus eaten. We're looking at what that floor on total crop tonnage does to the season. Plug it in and tell me where farm income lands.
expected_pyomo: model.total_prod_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.xprod[c] for c in model.c) >= 10.0)

query: Temporary labor spikes in peak months while family labor sits idle elsewhere. Shouldn't each month's temporary labor stay under 10% of the family-plus-temporary labor for that month? What's keeping the model from making that move?

constraints.name: avet
constraints.intent: Total veterinary cost is defined as the per-head veterinary service cost times the number of livestock.

constraints.name: income
constraints.intent: Farm income is revenue, plus the value of the food the family consumes itself, minus labor cost, ration cost, veterinary cost, and cropping cost, minus a risk penalty proportional to the average size of the yearly price deviations, counting positive and negative together, taken across all the years.

query: The plan keeps loading our scarce good land harder than the medium parcels for the same activity. Shouldn't each cropping activity keep its good-land hectares within a couple of hectares of its medium-land hectares, instead of leaning so hard on the prime ground? What's stopping the model from spreading each activity out like that?

model_narrative: A farm in the Agreste region plans a single season. It decides how many hectares of each cropping activity to run on each land type, how much livestock to keep under different feeding alternatives, how much of each crop to sell versus consume on-farm, how much family / temporary / permanent labor to use in each month, and how much land to downgrade between types. The goal is to maximize farm income.

sets.name: ps
sets.doc: set of feasible (cropping-activity p, land-type s) pairs (the activity/land combinations with positive crop yield)

params.name: rations
params.doc: livestock rations per feed alternative (cr per head)

params.name: lio
params.doc: land required per livestock feed alternative (ha per head)

params.name: llab
params.doc: labor for livestock feed (man-days per head)

params.name: crev
params.doc: crop revenue time series (cr per ha)

params.name: yield_param
params.doc: crop yields (kg per ha) — Pyomo attribute is model.yield_param ('yield' is a Python keyword)

params.name: ravg
params.doc: average crop revenue = mean over ty of crev(c,ty)

params.name: prdev
params.doc: price deviations for crops (cr per ton)

params.name: pcost
params.doc: cropping technology cost = sum over km of techc(p,km)

params.name: a
params.doc: cropping constant (0 if activity grows cotton-h, else 1)

vars.name: xcrop
vars.doc: cropping activities (ha); ub xcropl where set

vars.name: revenue
vars.doc: revenue from crop and livestock sales (cr)

constraints.name: sc:
constraints.intent: For each land type, the total area used cannot exceed the area of that type that is available. Land gets used in three ways: by growing crops on it, which only applies to land types suitable for cropping, by land that has been downgraded or converted in from another land type, and by land set aside to feed livestock.

constraints.name: lbal
constraints.intent: Total livestock raised equals the amount of livestock kept, summed across all the available feeding alternatives.

constraints.name: rliv
constraints.intent: The farm's total livestock ration requirement equals, summed over the feeding alternatives, the per-head ration cost of each alternative times the amount of livestock kept on that alternative.

constraints.name: yield_param[p,c,s]
constraints.intent: For each crop, the quantity produced must be at least the quantity used. Production is the crop's yield gathered across all cropping activities and land types, and usage is the amount of that crop sold plus the amount the family consumes through its consumption bundles.

constraints.name: xprod[c]
constraints.intent: For each crop, the total production in tons is the crop's yield summed over every cropping activity and land type.

constraints.name: labor[p,tm]
constraints.intent: For each month, the labor required cannot exceed the labor available that month. Labor is required by cropping activities, based on each activity's monthly labor need per hectare applied over the land it occupies, and by livestock feeding. The labor available that month is family labor plus temporary labor, plus permanent labor converted into man-days.

constraints.name: cond
constraints.intent: The family's on-farm consumption across the bundle alternatives must add up to exactly one full bundle.

constraints.name: prdev[c,ty]
constraints.intent: For each year, the revenue effect of that year's crop price deviations is the difference between a positive-deviation amount and a negative-deviation amount for that year. The revenue effect is each crop's price deviation for the year times the amount of that crop sold, summed over crops.

constraints.name: arev
constraints.intent: Total revenue is income from livestock sales plus income from crop sales. Livestock sales income is the livestock price times the number of head sold. Crop sales income is each crop's reference price times the amount of that crop sold, summed over crops.

constraints.name: acrop
constraints.intent: Total cropping cost is defined as, summed over every feasible pairing of a cropping activity with a land type, the activity's per-hectare technology cost times the hectares of that activity grown on that land.

constraints.name: alab
constraints.intent: Total labor cost is the cost of family labor and of temporary labor, each priced at its own wage rate and summed across the months, then converted from man-days to man-months, plus the cost of permanent labor at its own annual wage.

constraints.name: awcc
constraints.intent: The farm's cash spending during the season cannot exceed the available working capital. The cash items are cropping cost, livestock ration cost, veterinary cost, the wages paid for temporary labor across the months, and the wages paid for permanent labor. Family labor is not a cash outlay, so it is excluded here.

constraints.name: avet
constraints.intent: Total veterinary cost is defined as the per-head veterinary service cost times the number of livestock.

constraints.name: income
constraints.intent: Farm income is revenue, plus the value of the food the family consumes itself, minus labor cost, ration cost, veterinary cost, and cropping cost, minus a risk penalty proportional to the average size of the yearly price deviations, counting positive and negative together, taken across all the years.

query: We're putting most of our eggs in one basket on the sales side — one crop dominates the tonnage we ship. Shouldn't no single crop's sales run past 30% of everything we sell, so a price crash on one line doesn't sink the whole season? What's keeping the model from spreading the sales out?
